---
title: |
  | A Minimal Research Project Template
  | An Illustrative Example in Python
author:
  - name: Course Template
    email: your.name@example.com
    affiliation: Your University
date: last-modified
date-format: long
execute:
  echo: false
  message: false
  warning: false
abstract: |
  | \vfill This paper demonstrates a minimal reproducible Python workflow for class assignments. The project uses a tiny `mtcars` example, but preserves the basic logic of a larger empirical workflow: data are pulled, prepared, analyzed, serialized to disk, and then imported into the paper as prepared results.
  | \vspace{6cm}
bibliography: references.bib
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    number-sections: true
    toc: false
    fig-cap-location: top
fontsize: 11pt
ident: yes
header-includes:
  - \usepackage[nolists]{endfloat}
  - \usepackage{setspace}\doublespacing
  - \setlength{\parindent}{4em}
  - \setlength{\parskip}{0em}
  - \usepackage[hang,flushmargin]{footmisc}
  - \usepackage{caption}
  - \captionsetup[table]{skip=12pt,font=bf}
  - \captionsetup[figure]{skip=12pt,font=bf}
  - \usepackage{array}
  - \usepackage{threeparttable}
  - \usepackage{adjustbox}
  - \usepackage{graphicx}
  - \usepackage{csquotes}
  - \usepackage[margin=1in]{geometry}
  - \usepackage{booktabs}
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

import pickle
from pathlib import Path
from IPython.display import Image, display

results_path = Path("../output/rct-project-template-results.pkl")
with results_path.open("rb") as results_file:
    results = pickle.load(results_file)

descriptive_table = results["descriptive_table"]
scatter_figure_path = (Path("..") / results["scatter_figure_path"]).resolve()
table_note = results["table_note"]
highlights = results["highlights"]


def render_table(data, note):
  table_lines = [
      "\\begin{tabular}[t]{lrrrr}",
      "\\toprule",
      " & ".join(str(column) for column in data.columns) + "\\\\",
      "\\midrule"
  ]

  for row in data.itertuples(index=False, name=None):
    rendered_cells = []
    for value in row:
      value = value.item() if hasattr(value, "item") else value
      if isinstance(value, float):
        rendered_cells.append(f"{value:.1f}")
      else:
        rendered_cells.append(str(value))
    table_lines.append(" & ".join(rendered_cells) + "\\\\")

  table_lines.extend([
      "\\bottomrule",
      "\\end{tabular}"
  ])

  latex_table = [
    "\\begin{threeparttable}",
    *table_lines,
    "\\begin{tablenotes}[flushleft]",
    "\\setlength{\\labelsep}{0pt}",
    f"\\item \\emph{{Note}}: {note}",
    "\\end{tablenotes}",
    "\\end{threeparttable}"
  ]

  print("\n".join(latex_table))
```

\pagebreak

# Introduction

This repository is the Python version of a barebones project template for class assignments. It keeps the moving parts of a larger workflow visible while staying small enough to understand in one sitting. Data move through a pull step, a preparation step, an analysis step, and finally into a paper that reads saved results rather than re-running the analytical pipeline. The paper structure is intentionally compact, but it already includes standard scholarly features such as citations and a bibliography [@Wickham2014].

# Data

The example uses the built-in `mtcars` dataset that ships with `plotnine`. In this template, `code/python/pull_data.py` writes a raw object to `data/pulled/`, `code/python/prep_data.py` creates a prepared analysis dataset in `data/generated/`, and `code/python/run_analysis.py` reads that prepared dataset to create the final analytical objects stored in `output/`.

```{python}
#| output: asis
print(
    f"The prepared sample contains {highlights['sample_size']} observations. "
    f"Average fuel efficiency is {highlights['avg_mpg']} miles per gallon, "
    f"average horsepower is {highlights['avg_horsepower']}, and the heaviest "
    f"vehicle in the sample is {highlights['heaviest_model']}."
)
```

# Analysis

The analysis step estimates a simple linear model of miles per gallon as a function of vehicle weight and transmission type. The goal is not substantive complexity. The goal is to show how a project can separate raw data handling, preparation, analysis, and reporting into distinct scripts that produce durable artifacts.

# Results

```{python}
#| label: tbl-summary
#| tbl-cap: Descriptive statistics by transmission show that manual cars in the prepared sample are lighter and more fuel efficient on average.
#| output: asis
render_table(descriptive_table, table_note)
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-weight
#| fig-cap: Fuel efficiency declines with vehicle weight in the prepared sample, with manual cars concentrated among the lighter and more efficient models.
#| out-width: 85%
display(Image(filename=str(scatter_figure_path)))
```

```{python}
#| output: asis
print(
    f"The fitted model implies a {highlights['weight_slope']} mpg change for "
    f"an additional 1,000 pounds of vehicle weight. The coefficient on manual "
    f"transmission is {highlights['manual_effect']} mpg. "
    f"{highlights['interpretation']}"
)
```

# Conclusion

This repository is intentionally small, but it is meant to be built on. Students can replace the toy data step, extend the preparation logic, add richer analysis, and continue writing in the same paper structure without changing the overall workflow shape.

A visible placeholder remains here for the in-class GitHub edit if needed: `YOUR NAME – summary`.

\pagebreak

# References {-}
\setlength{\parindent}{-0.2in}
\setlength{\leftskip}{0.2in}
\setlength{\parskip}{8pt}
\noindent
